paragraphs.text: FlaxDigitalPolicy

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
About Flux
paragraphs.text: Flux Digital Policy is a policy consultancy specialising in the video games sector. As well as supporting clients with navigating regulatory frameworks and engaging in policy development, we also participate in our own capacity. With senior staff from a consumer protection regulation background, we know how important background information can be when it comes to designing effective and proportionate interventions. Our response is intended to provide details on some of the specific facets of the video games industry that must be thoroughly considered during the development of the upcoming Digital Fairness Act. We are not representing a client, and all views expressed in this document are our own.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Summary
paragraphs.text: We support the aims of the European Commission to better protect consumers from unfair practices online through the DFA. However, we believe that this could be most effectively achieved through the existing principles and provisions of existing instruments, such as the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, rather than through new legislation. We have concerns about the potential for some of the suggested actions to pose a risk of detriment to consumers and believe that these approaches may not fit the contours of the video games industry. Overall, we consider that a principles-based approach, accompanied by sector-specific guidance drawn up in consultation with industry, and enforced on a case-by-case basis, will create the best environment for consumers.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
The diversity of the video games industry
paragraphs.text: To be effective and proportionate, regulation in the video games industry must recognise its inherent diversity: a vast array of content, all with unique contexts and attributes. This complexity is amplified by a wide range of approaches to monetising games and how these practices are embedded within them. Variances in game design and commercial implementation can have a big impact on what fairness and transparency look like in any one game. This means that regulation must be designed to work within a complex sector.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
The diversity of the video games industry
paragraphs.text: The complexity of the sector does not in itself pose a risk of detriment to consumers; they are not navigating the ecosystem as a whole, but engaging with each game on its own terms. What matters, then, is that each individual game is fair and transparent in its commercial practices. This breadth of possibilities for gameplay, monetisation, and communication design entails an approach to regulation that can encompass many different means of dealing fairly with consumers.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
The diversity of the video games industry
paragraphs.text: Given this diversity, consumers will best be served by a regulatory approach that maintains the principles of existing regulations, particularly UCPD, since this can accommodate the degree to

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
The diversity of the video games industry
paragraphs.text: which each game can differ from the next. To give clarity to industry and confidence to enforcement bodies, this will necessitate the development of sector-specific guidance for the video games industry. It is imperative that this is created in partnership with the industry so that it can reflect the ability of companies to tailor their compliance approach to best suit their players. Without this engagement, there is a risk that the regulatory position could fail to account for material differences in gameplay contexts, making it more difficult to improve consumer outcomes and, for reasons set out further below, could even worsen them.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Digital features – in-game currencies
paragraphs.text: In-game currencies are called out for specific attention in the consultation questionnaire, and we understand that the DFA seeks to respond to concerns about their potential impact on consumer behaviour.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Digital features – in-game currencies
paragraphs.text: While some implementations of in-game currencies could pose risks, in-game currencies are not inherently unfair, and not all implementations are harmful to consumers. We recognise the value of surveys and consultations in highlighting areas for further assessment, but significant regulatory interventions must be founded on robust, empirical evidence. In our view, current evidence is not sufficient to support the position that in-game currencies distort economic behaviour as a matter of course, and it therefore cannot underpin blanket regulatory measures. In the absence of this evidence, regulation needs to focus on the outcome of the commercial practice – how in-game currencies can be implemented in a fair and transparent manner – rather than targeting the mechanic itself.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Benefits of in-game currencies
paragraphs.text: Where this is achieved, in-game currencies are not only harmless, they can also confer significant consumer benefits.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Benefits of in-game currencies
paragraphs.text: In-game currencies can remove transactions from active gameplay: consumers make an initial decision to purchase a specific amount to 'take into' the active gameplay environment as an in-game resource. If they use up this resource and want to buy more, they must exit active gameplay and go back to the storefront. The subsequent decision to spend more money on additional in-game currency therefore takes place in a context outside of the immediate pressures of gameplay. From this feature, specific benefits are realised:

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Benefits of in-game currencies
paragraphs.text: Conscious spending control – the separation of real-world spending and gameplay detaches players from the impulse-driven context of the game, creating a helpful layer of friction. Pre-purchasing a set amount of in-game currency prevents players from accidentally over-spending. Once the chosen amount of in-game currency has been used, any decision to spend more must be deliberate (further reinforced by disrupting gameplay and removing the impulsive context).

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Benefits of in-game currencies
paragraphs.text: Parental boundaries – hard spending caps may not be the best fit for all families' approach to in-game spending, but in-game currencies allow a flexible approach to limiting children's spending through the pre-purchase of a set amount. This amount can be tailored to the specific play-session and budget, providing guardrails within which the child can be given free rein without the risk of overspend.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Benefits of in-game currencies
paragraphs.text: Distinguishing commercial practices – as children find it more difficult to distinguish between gameplay content and commercial messaging, in-game currencies are a helpful tool to create concrete separation between the two. By using in-game currencies, the commercial practices are walled-off from gameplay, making it straightforward for children to avoid accidental spending.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Benefits of in-game currencies
paragraphs.text: There are also positives from a broader player experience perspective. The use of in-game currencies supports the ability for players to earn the currency (rather than just purchase it) and therefore still have access to the same content as those who choose to spend money. This, in turn, opens up the potential player base for a game, creating better market conditions for new entrants as well as benefiting players who cannot otherwise afford to participate, which is crucial for democratising access to one of the most popular forms of entertainment among Europeans.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Benefits of in-game currencies
paragraphs.text: Moreover, SME developers in particular benefit from implementing in-game currencies, since it simplifies the integration of transactions into the game. Instead of making multiple small purchases of individual items, players can make a single transaction for in-game currency. As transactions are usually routed through 3rd party providers, streamlining the logistics and reducing transaction fees lessens the development burden. Ultimately, this creates a more favourable environment where new businesses can be more confident of success.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: We understand that there are concerns about consumers' ability to understand how much real-world money they will need to spend to obtain in-game content if in-game currencies are used. However, this is not an inherent problem with in-game currency as a concept – it is absolutely feasible for in-game currencies to be implemented in ways that are fair and transparent, without the need for fiat price statements.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: Pricing transparency is not merely about displaying a fiat currency statement by an item: it's about ensuring that players are clear on the real-world cost of obtaining an item. In many instances, this would not be achieved through fiat price statements (and could, in fact, be worsened by them).

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: To understand how much real-world money is required to obtain an in-game item, players need to know how much in-game currency they must purchase and how much that will cost. Critically, this will not always be the same for every player – it depends on their specific circumstances, such as how much in-game currency they already have and whether they intend to buy a large currency bundle to gain a quantity discount and fund future purchases. For some players, they may not even need to spend any money because they intend to earn the requisite currency through gameplay.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: This scenario represents a clear example of a product with a price that cannot be calculated in advance, already addressed under UCPD. In such cases, the player must instead be provided with the information needed to calculate the price.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: There are many ways to provide this information, but among the most straightforward to implement is to show the player:

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: How much in-game currency they have,

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: The total needed to obtain the item (i.e. its in-game currency price), and

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: Clear and easy access to the currency-purchasing page so they can select whatever amount is suitable for their needs.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: This ensures that the player is always able to easily determine the real-world cost to them of obtaining the in-game content at that point. Other approaches could include indicative pricing where the basis is relevant (such as the lowest available bundle cost) and made clear to the player as to whether it accounts for their current balance.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Fairness and transparency
paragraphs.text: As the price of in-game content is often not a fixed point, we have concerns that requiring a flat currency price statement could mislead consumers about the actual amount of real-world money they need to spend. In many cases, such statements are likely to overstate the cost, particularly where players may earn in-game currency. Since both overstatement and understatement of price can distort consumer behaviour, mandating flat currency prices carries a significant risk of requiring misleading price statements.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Flexible regulation
paragraphs.text: Flux considers that in-game currency can be part of a fair commercial framework and that it would be disproportionate to restrict its use outright, or to mandate requirements that risk distorting consumer understanding.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Flexible regulation
paragraphs.text: It is therefore important for regulation in this area to be flexible enough to prohibit unfair practices while also enabling fair and responsible implementation of in-game currencies. A principles-based approach to regulation is a well-established and effective method of addressing practices where the impacts are heavily affected by context and execution. Given that pricing transparency is already covered by UCPD, including scenarios where a fixed price cannot be calculated, we consider that there is sufficient existing regulation in this area.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Flexible regulation
paragraphs.text: As such, the strongest way forward would be to develop sector-specific guidance on this topic. To ensure that it provides the maximum benefit to consumers and does not place disproportionate limits on game design, it is vital that this guidance should be developed through engagement with industry and subject to public consultation.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Simplification and principles-based regulation
paragraphs.text: We strongly agree with the aim to ensure that regulation is as simple and straightforward as possible, provided that it is still effective and proportionate – simplification should not be prioritised for its own sake. Complex regulation creates information asymmetry for consumers, making it hard to understand and exercise their rights, and imposes high compliance costs and regulatory uncertainty on businesses, particularly in highly dynamic and competitive industries such as video games. Furthermore, fragmentation complicates enforcement for authorities due to overlapping competencies and overstretched resources.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Simplification and principles-based regulation
paragraphs.text: Balancing enhanced consumer protection with the simultaneous reduction of legal and administrative burdens on businesses will no doubt be a challenging goal for the forthcoming DFA. To meet this challenge the Commission should prioritise the use of existing legislation over the development of new rules, focusing on sector-specific guidance to promote clarity and certainty for consumers, businesses, and enforcement authorities alike. Simplification can be achieved by communicating standards clearly, transparently, and consistently, enabling them to be applied flexibly but predictably, both across and within all sectors.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Simplification and principles-based regulation
paragraphs.text: Instead of adding further prescriptive rules to an already complex landscape, we believe the optimal path forward is to promote simplification and streamlining through a principles-based regulatory approach. Retaining and making full use of existing core principles (like those in the UCPD) rather than enacting new prescriptive legislation, offers the most viable solution to enhance consumer protection effectively while ensuring compliance is proportionate and predictable.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Simplification and principles-based regulation
paragraphs.text: With most of the Commission's concerns already addressed by existing consumer protection principles, the priority should be to ensure that traders and regulators are equipped to understand how these apply in digital contexts. This entails developing clear and comprehensive guidance, tailored to avoid the risk of adverse consumer outcomes like those outlined in our response. As we have noted elsewhere, by collaborating with industry and consulting publicly, the Commission can produce resources that reflect diverse commercial environments and support better consumer understanding. This also supports more cohesive enforcement, helping regulators to remain aligned and consistent across a wide variety of digital products and services.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Simplification and principles-based regulation
paragraphs.text: Significantly, these existing regulations already provide the flexible, principles-based approach to consumer protection that is so vital for diverse sectors such as the video games industry. In our experience, principles-based consumer protection regulation is effective, practical, and straightforward to enforce. Because the focus is on the outcome, it can move with new developments as they happen, rather than restrict innovation. It enables the widest possible range of approaches to dealing fairly with consumers, while prohibiting the use of practices that can never be fair. In an area like consumer protection, where so many products and services fall into scope, it is essential to have a regulatory framework that is built to adapt to new products, new markets, and new contexts.
